text: In real life, we approach the right chest using three ports.
Cameras stayed in the inferior port all the time, which is our routine strategy.
We then harvested station 10, station 7 and we are about to start harvesting stations 2 to 4.
The vagus nerve is then slung using a sloop or a suture of your choice and retracted out of the way.
The azicus arch is similarly slooped and retracted downwards to expose the tracheobronchial junction which is our landmark for the carina.
This should be just above the azicus arch.
A ring forceps is then dug just behind the trachea at that point and opened widely.
This will be the tunnel that will lead us to our target node.
And indeed, once the space between the trachea and esophagus is opened, the target node is clearly seen without a shred of doubt.
The node is then teased out with the help of an endopinot and an N-seal bipolar dissection.
The NC energy device should be used very close to the node to avoid injury to nerves.
Bleeding from the feeding vessel is not usually a problem and can be easily controlled.
At the end of harvesting, the space is checked for bleeding and hemostasis is secured before closing the ports over a single 28 French drain.
In practice, getting to this space requires disconnection of the ligamentum arteriosum.
The latter is only clearly seen when all the nodes in station 5 and 6 are cleared.
Note is made of the recurrent laryngeal nerve separating from the vagus over the arch of the aorta and running to the right side of it, disappearing below the ligamentum.
Again, we should only be mindful of the motor branch of the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The ligamentum arteriosum is then dissected clearly and lifted with a vascular sloop before firing a stapling device.
This will expose the subiotic space and brings the main stem trachea well into display.
Only then the precarinal and the pretracheal space are accessible.
A distinction is made between what is lateral to the lateral border of the trachea and what is anterior to it, and this will lead to correct identification of targeted 4R nodes.
Obviously, 4L and 5L are also easily accessible within this view.
The main caveat about dissection in this area is preserving the motor branch of the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
